coredata.dc:title: Adhered-3D paper microfluidic analytical device based on oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-doped carbon dots nanozyme for point-of-care testing of alkaline phosphatase
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jing
Zhang, Jing
Tong, Xia
coredata.dc:description: Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) have become promising alternatives to clinical laboratory-based methods for point-of-care testing (POCT) of biomarkers in family care and resource-limited communities. Here, Co-doped carbon dots (Co-CDs) nanozyme with outstanding oxidase-mimicking catalytic activity and red fluorescent emission were prepared, and combined adhered-3D μPAD (A-3D μPAD) to monitor facilely alkaline phosphatase (ALP) level in whole blood samples. Co-CDs catalyzed the oxidization of nonfluorescent o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) with yellow fluorescent emission due to the generation of tremendous O2 •- species. With addition of ALP, ALP hydrolyzed l-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate into ascorbic acid, and the latter was oxidized by Co-CDs, then reacted with OPD to form blue fluorescent emission 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo [3,4-b]quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ). Both DFQ and oxOPD quenched the fluorescence intensity of Co-CDs via inner-filter effect. The cascade reaction of ALP/Co-CDs was incorporated into A-3D μPAD based on above sensing principles. A-3D μPAD enabled sample pretreatment, cascade reaction and signal output, and integrated portable minimized device and smartphone for visual ALP detection. The linear range and limit of detection for ALP were 0.5–150 U L−1 and 0.1 U L−1, respectively, and the color varied from red, yellow to blue. The detection results for whole blood samples were consistent with biochemical detector. The efficiency, disposability, practicality and low-cost of A-3D μPAD can be extended to determine various biomarkers, and provided technical support for nanozyme applications in POCT environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co-doped carbon dots
Oxidase-mimicking nanozyme
Fluorometric
Adhered-3D paper-based microfluidic analytical device
Alkaline phosphatase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and fabrication of the A-3D μPAD
ALP assay based on A-3D μPAD
The performance of A-3D μPAD for ALP detection
Detection of ALP activity in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202303021212159 and No. 202303021222319). This research was also supported by the Science Fundation of Shanxi Bethune Hospital (No. 2023GZRZ19 and No. 2023RC02).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fabrication process of the hydrophobic barrier on WFP-5 or PSM. (B) The structure of A-3D μPAD for ALP activity assay.

(A) TEM image of the Co-CDs (Inset: the size distribution of Co-CDs). (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of Co-CDs (Insets: Co-CDs images under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp, respectively). (C) FT-IR spectra of Co-CDs and CDs. (D) XPS survey spectra of Co-CDs and CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs + OPD, Co-CDs, Co-CDs + OPD and OPD. (B) The ESR spectra of DMPO, and DMPO in the presence of CDs and Co-CDs, respectively.
(A) Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of Co-CDs, and UV–vis absorption spectra of DFQ and oxOPD, respectively. (B) The fluorescent lifetime of Co-CDs, Co-CDs + DFQ and Co-CDs + oxOPD.

The calibration curve between B/R ratio and ALP activity (Inset: the representative images of A-3D μPAD with ALP activity ranging from 0 to 150 U L−1). Error bars represent the standard deviation of parallel measurements (n = 3).

The B/R ratios of A-3D μPAD for interfering substances in Co-CDs/OPD/AAP system (A) and Co-CDs/OPD/ALP/AAP system (B) for ALP detection. The concentration of metal ions, anion and small molecules was 100 mM, the activity of biomacromolecules was 1000 U L−1 and ALP activity was set at 100 U L−1. Error bars represent the standard deviation of parallel measurements (n = 3).
Schematic diagram of working principles of Co-CDs/OPD system incorporating into A-3D μPAD for sensitive ALP detection via smartphone.

head.ce:title: Adhered-3D paper microfluidic analytical device based on oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-doped carbon dots nanozyme for point-of-care testing of alkaline phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Information Engineering, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 031000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Information Engineering, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 031000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 031000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 031000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Stem Cell Translation Laboratory, Shanxi Bethune Hospital, Shanxi Academy of Medical Sciences, Third Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Tongji Shanxi Hospital, Taiyuan, 030032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Third Hospital of Shanxi Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Stem Cell Translation Laboratory, Shanxi Bethune Hospital, Shanxi Academy of Medical Sciences, Third Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Tongji Shanxi Hospital, Taiyuan, 030032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) have become promising alternatives to clinical laboratory-based methods for point-of-care testing (POCT) of biomarkers in family care and resource-limited communities. Here, Co-doped carbon dots (Co-CDs) nanozyme with outstanding oxidase-mimicking catalytic activity and red fluorescent emission were prepared, and combined adhered-3D μPAD (A-3D μPAD) to monitor facilely alkaline phosphatase (ALP) level in whole blood samples. Co-CDs catalyzed the oxidization of nonfluorescent o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) with yellow fluorescent emission due to the generation of tremendous O2 •- species. With addition of ALP, ALP hydrolyzed l-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate into ascorbic acid, and the latter was oxidized by Co-CDs, then reacted with OPD to form blue fluorescent emission 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo [3,4-b]quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ). Both DFQ and oxOPD quenched the fluorescence intensity of Co-CDs via inner-filter effect. The cascade reaction of ALP/Co-CDs was incorporated into A-3D μPAD based on above sensing principles. A-3D μPAD enabled sample pretreatment, cascade reaction and signal output, and integrated portable minimized device and smartphone for visual ALP detection. The linear range and limit of detection for ALP were 0.5–150 U L−1 and 0.1 U L−1, respectively, and the color varied from red, yellow to blue. The detection results for whole blood samples were consistent with biochemical detector. The efficiency, disposability, practicality and low-cost of A-3D μPAD can be extended to determine various biomarkers, and provided technical support for nanozyme applications in POCT environments.

An efficiency, disposability, practicality and low-cost of A-3D μPAD platform was explored for sensitive and facile ALP detection in whole blood samples.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a crucial hydrolase to catalyze the dephosphorylation of biomolecules such as nucleic acids and proteins [1,2]. ALP distributes widely in human organs including the liver, intestine and bone, and has been considered as an important biomarker in disease diagnosis [3,4]. The elevated level of ALP is closely associated with hepatopathy, osteopathy, diabetes and cancers, and the depressed level is related to leukemia and thyroid diseases [2,5]. Various methods have been developed to monitor ALP activity including electrochemistry, colorimetry, fluorometry, high performance liquid chromatography and surface-enhanced Raman scattering-based techniques, while the large analytical instruments are generally required for professional operators [6,7]. Colorimetric and fluorometric-based methods are employed widely with the merits of facile operation and visual phenomena. However, colorimetric results are affected easily by the color of biological fluids such as red blood cells [8]. Fluorescent methods have attracted much attentions due to their simplicity, sensitivity and high-throughput analysis [9]. Especially, red and near-infrared fluorescent emissions take advantages of weak auto-fluorescence, low light absorption and deep-tissue penetration [10]. While they also face great challenges such as complex sample preparation (e.g. centrifugation, desorption), sophisticated instruments and well-trained operators [3,8]. Therefore, it is significant to develop a reliable, low-cost, equipment-free and user-friendly platform for point-of-care testing (POCT) of ALP activity to promote diseases diagnosis, especially for family care and remote areas.

Comparing to laboratory-based tests, POCT has gained much interest because of smart device, simplified procedure and digitized readout [11,12]. Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) with the advantages of paper availability, easy fabrication, capillary driving force and storage capability are potential candidates for POCT [13,14]. The 2D μPADs consist of hydrophilic channels (e.g. chromatographic paper, filter paper) and hydrophobic barriers (e.g. wax/laser/thermal printing, plotting), and 3D μPADs are fabricated via origami, lamination and alignment of 2D μPADs [15,16]. The sample extraction and bioanalysis feature of 2D and 3D μPADs supply powerful platforms for clinical and biomedical applications [11,14]. While, μPADs commonly suffer from low sensitivity, researchers have devoted to amplifying the visual detection signals to improve sensitivity as follows [15]. Introducing new materials (e.g. gold nanoparticles, nanocontainers), sample preconcentration techniques, multiple cascade reactions and more sensitive sensing strategies (e.g. fluorescent, chemiluminescent) have been verified to increase efficiently the sensitivity of μPADs [12,17]. The architecture of 2D μPADs changes to 3D μPADs with multiplexed analysis capability can also enhance sensitivity [12,18]. In our previous studies, origami and lab-in-a-syringe 3D μPADs have been developed successfully for ALP measurements with high sensitivity [19,20]. Therefore, μPADs offer sensitive, accurate and easy-to-use POCT platforms for ALP detection to advance precision and personalized medicine, especially for resource-limited communities.

Nanozymes with enzyme-mimicking characteristics have the advantages of easy preparation, high catalytic efficiency, good stability and adjustable activity compared to natural enzymes [21,22]. Numerous nanozymes have been reported to exhibit inherent oxidase-, peroxidase- and catalase-mimicking activities and applied in the fields of biosensing, biomedicine, food safety and environmental protection [23–25]. The oxidase-mimicking nanozymes such as single-atom catalysts, metal-organic frameworks, carbon-based nanomaterials and composite nanomaterials have been widely explored to develop various biosensors [26]. Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of typical carbon-based nanomaterial, with excellent photoluminescent property, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility have been used extensively in sensing and bioimaging domains [27,28]. Recently, CDs have been reported to possess oxidase-mimicking activity to construct sensitive and accurate sensing platforms for nitrite, pesticides and antibiotics detection [22,24,25]. Metal element doped (e.g. Mn-, Co-, Pt-) and surface modification (e.g. physisorption, covalent coating) of CDs can provide more binding sites to enhance remarkably catalytic activity [29–31]. Combining the high oxidase-mimicking activity and excellent fluorescent property of CDs can achieve photo-responsive nanozyme sensing platform via a reasonable design.

In this research, we developed a multi-emitting fluorescent platform by Co-doped CDs (Co-CDs)/o-phenylenediamine (OPD) system and combined adhered-3D μPAD (A-3D μPAD) for visual ALP detection in human whole blood (Scheme 1 ). The red emission Co-CDs were prepared using neutral red (NR), urea, CoCl2 as precursors via one-step hydrothermal method. The Co (II) active sites in Co-CDs provide excellent oxidase-mimicking activity to catalyze non-fluorescent OPD to 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) with yellow fluorescent emission. In the presence of ALP, l-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) is hydrolyzed to ascorbic acid (AA) which is oxidized into dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) by Co-CDs and DHAA reacts with OPD forming 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo [3,4-b]quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ) with blue fluorescent emission. Both DFQ and oxOPD can quench the fluorescent intensity of Co-CDs via the inner filter effect (IFE), and ALP monitor was fabricated according to above principles. Integrating Co-CDs/OPD system into A-3D μPAD for POCT of ALP activity, the detection zones displayed distinct fluorescent signal output in red-yellow-blue manner with the increasing of ALP activity. The corresponding images were captured and converted to red (R), green (G) and blue (B) values by the “Color Recognition” application in smartphone to acquire ALP activity. With the superior oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-CDs nanozyme and reliable A-3D μPAD, a feasible, sensitive, low-cost platform (0.395 RMB) was constructed for on-site analysis of ALP activity in whole blood samples.
Details of the reagents and apparatus are described in Supplementary Information.

Briefly, NR (10 mg), urea (10 mg) and CoCl2 (20 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture were transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 2 h. The pink-colored product was dialyzed (MWCO 500) for 2 days, filtered (0.22 μm) and freeze-dried to obtain Co-CDs product. For comparison, no Co-doping CDs were synthesized using only NR (10 mg) and urea (10 mg) as precursors by the same procedure with Co-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of the A-3D μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the hydrophilic Whatman filter paper No. 5 (WFP-5, 8 mm diameter) or plasma separation membrane (PSM) was sandwiched between steel mold (6 mm diameter) and magnetic sheet (100 mm × 100 mm). The black oil-based marker was installed in electric airbrush, and turned on to spray black pigment to penetrate through WFP-5 for 5 s, and the ring pattern of hydrophobic barrier was obtained. The whole A-3D μPAD includes three 2D μPADs (Fig. 1B). The upper layer is PSM to separate serum from whole blood sample, and both interlayer and bottom layers are WFP-5. In Table S1, the fabricating cost of A-3D μPAD is about 0.395 (RMB). In order to avoid the direct reaction between Co-CDs and the chromogenic reagent of OPD, OPD (100 mM, 6 μL) was pipetted onto the reagent zone of interlayer and dried at 25 °C for 10 min. Co-CDs (1.25 mg mL−1, 6 μL) was added on bottom layer drying for 10 min at 25 °C, sequentially AAP (75 mM, 6 μL) in Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 8.5) was introduced drying for 10 min at 25 °C. Then, three layers are adhered together via double-sided tape to construct A-3D μPAD. For practical testing, the prepared A-3D μPAD was kept at −20 °C in dark place for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP assay based on A-3D μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: For ALP assay, a series of ALP standards solution (0–150 U L−1) were prepared with H2O. 18 μL of each standard solution was added to make ALP on upper layer and OPD on interlayer flow to bottom layer. Then, the chemical reactions among ALP, AAP, Co-CDs and OPD were triggered on bottom layer. The A-3D μPAD was set in incubator at 37 °C for 25 min. Finally, the A-3D μPAD was turned upside down, and placed vertically below the UV lamp (365 nm) in the portable device (Fig. S1). The images were captured by smartphone and analyzed to obtain RGB values of detection zone with “Color Recognition” application. The LOD is calculated according to the equation, LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation for the B/R values of ten independent measurements of detection system via A-3D μPAD in the absence of ALP. S is the slope of fitting equation of ALP activity and B/R values.

Co-CDs were synthesized using NR, urea, CoCl2 as precursors via hydrothermal method. With the precursor mass ratios (NR to CoCl2) ranging from 1:0.5 to 1:2.5, oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-CDs rises, while the fluorescence intensity reaches maximum at 1:2.0 (Fig. S2). Thus, the suitable mass ratio was set as 1:2.0 in the consideration of the oxidase-mimicking activity and fluorescence intensity of Co-CDs. In Fig. 2 A, the transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of Co-CDs exhibits spherical particles and well disperses with an average diameter of 3.25 ± 0.64 nm. The X-ray diffraction pattern of Co-CDs shows a diffraction peak at 2θ = 26° indicating the amorphous nature of Co-CDs (Fig. S3A) [32]. In UV–vis spectrum of Co-CDs (Fig. 2B), the absorption bands at 253 nm and 276 nm are attributed to π-π∗ transition of CC, and the peaks at 343 nm and 489 nm are from n-π∗ transition of CO and CN on the surface of Co-CDs [33]. Under the 365 nm excitation for Co-CDs, a strong red emission spectrum centered at 609 nm is obtained. The Co-CDs solution displays pale-orange color under daylight, and bright red fluorescent under UV lamp (365 nm). In Fig. S3B, Co-CDs exhibit excitation-independent property with the excitation wavelengths from 340 to 385 nm illustrating that Co-CDs have uniform nanostructure and luminescence center [21,34]. The quantum yield of Co-CDs is measured to be 13.8 % using rhodamine B as reference [35].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Co-CDs and CDs are shown in Fig. 2C, the characteristic peaks at around 3300, 2800, 1600, 1440 and 1052 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of O–H/N–H, C–H, CO/CN, C–N and C–O–C, respectively [36]. It suggests the surface of Co-CDs and CDs contains hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl functional groups. Comparing with CDs, the absorption band of Co-CDs at 527 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of Co–N, which indicates the successful doping of cobalt (II) to provide the active site for Co-CDs [21]. The chemical composition and surface chemical states of Co-CDs and CDs were performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2D, the C, N and O element peaks are observed in both Co-CDs and CDs spectra, while Co 2p peak is only observed notably in Co-CDs demonstrating the introduction of Co element in Co-CDs. In XPS spectrum of Co-CDs, the peaks at 780.90, 532.76, 399.58 and 285.55 eV are assigned to Co 2p (0.86 %), O 1s (22.82 %), N 1s (12.94 %), and C 1s (63.38 %), respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Co 2p displays doublet at 796.78 and 781.08 eV corresponding to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 with two satellite peaks at 786.28 and 802.78 eV, respectively, which indicates the presence of Co2+ in Co-CDs structure (Fig. S3C) [37,38]. In Fig. S3D, the O 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to C–O (533.58 eV) and CO (532.28 eV), respectively [39]. The high resolved N 1s spectrum reveals three peaks at 400.88, 399.98 and 399.08 eV indicating the presence of graphitic N, pyrrole N and pyridinic N (Fig. S3E) [39,40]. The C 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks centered at 286.78, 285.88 and 284.88 eV assigning to CO, C–O/C–N and C–C/CC, respectively (Fig. S3F) [36]. As a result, Co-CDs were successfully synthesized for further experiments.

To investigate the oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-CDs, OPD was employed as chromogenic substrate. In Fig. 3 A, the increasing absorption signal in Co-CDs/OPD system is corresponding to the absorption peak of oxOPD (λ max 449 nm) due to the oxOPD formation via the reaction of OPD reacting with dissolved O2 in H2O catalyzed by Co-CDs [34]. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescent spectrum of Co-CDs/OPD system appears characteristic emission of oxOPD at 565 nm. Under the same conditions, CDs/OPD system appears no new peak in UV–vis and fluorescent spectra. It implies that CDs display no oxidase-mimicking activity to trigger the oxidization of OPD to produce oxOPD. The steady-state kinetics of Co-CDs was evaluated by Michaelis-Menten equation: ν = Vmax × [S]/(Km + [S]), the Vmax and Km represented the maximal reaction velocity and Michaelis constant respectively. In Fig. S5A, the catalytic velocity elevates steadily and then reaches plateau with the increase of OPD concentration, and follows well with Michaelis-Menten model. According to the Lineweaver-Burk equation (1)/ν = (Km/Vmax) (1/[S]) + 1/Vmax, the Vmax and Km of Co-CDs for OPD are calculated to be 8.64 × 10−8 M s−1 and 7.08 mM, respectively (Fig. S5B).

To further investigate the catalytic mechanism of Co-CDs/OPD system, reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenger experiments and Electron spin resonance (ESR) characterization were proceeded. The sodium azide (NaN3), t-butanol (TBA) and 1,4-benzoquinone (PBQ) were selected as scavengers for 1O2, •OH and O2 •-, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6, only PBQ could inhibit significantly the absorbance intensity comparing with other two scavengers indicating that O2 •- is mainly responsible for the oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-CDs. 5,5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a trapping agent for radicals in ESR measurement. As shown in Fig. 3B, the ESR spectrum of Co-CDs/DMPO system has six distinct peaks corresponding to DMPO-O2 •- adduct. It suggests that Co-CDs as oxidase-like nanozyme activate dissolved O2 to generate tremendous O2 •- radicals [22,41]. The high oxidative property of O2 •- radical oxidizes OPD to form oxOPD. For comparison, no DMPO- O2 •- signal is detected in ESR spectrum of CDs/DMPO system. The results of UV–vis, fluorescent, ROS scavenger and ESR measurements verify that the remarkable oxidase-like activity of intrinsic Co-CDs originates from the doped Co (II).

The catalytic activity of Co-CDs nanozyme also shows pH dependence similar to other oxidase-mimicking nanozymes, and maximum oxidase-mimicking activity is acquired at pH 8.5 (Fig. S7A). As shown in Fig. S7B, Co-CDs still maintain high oxidase-mimicking activity (over 90 %) even after 70 days. It indicates that Co-CDs have outstanding storage stability and benefits for the sensing application in POCT. In addition, 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were used as chromogenic substrates to test the oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-CDs. As displayed in Figs. S7C and D, Co-CDs + ABTS and Co-CDs + TMB systems show no characteristic absorption peaks at 417 nm and 652 nm, respectively. It is possible that the electrostatic repulsion of negatively charged ABTS and low solubility of TMB decrease the reaction ratio between chromogenic substrates and Co-CDs [38].

Inspired by the excellent oxidase-mimicking activity of Co-CDs, a fluorescent-traffic-light sensor was proposed for ALP assay (Fig. S8A). With addition of ALP, ALP hydrolyzes AAP to form AA, and AA is oxidized to DHAA by the oxidase-mimicking of Co-CDs nanozyme with red emission at 609 nm. DHAA reacts with OPD to yield DFQ with blue fluorescent emission at 430 nm (Fig. S8B). In the absence of ALP, the generation of AA is prevented. Co-CDs catalyze the oxidization of OPD to oxOPD with yellow fluorescent emission at 565 nm. In Fig. S8C, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm gradually rises, the intensity at 565 nm decreases, and the intensity at 609 nm reduces with ALP activity ranging from 0 to 150 U L−1. In UV–vis spectra, the characteristic absorption peaks of DFQ and oxOPD at 330 nm and 449 nm are observed (Fig. S8D). The possible oxidization mechanism of Co-CDs for ALP sensing is shown in Fig. S8E. Co-CDs oxidizes dissolved O2 to generate O2 •- radicals, and AA or OPD is oxidized by O2 •- to form the corresponding products.

In order to understand the sensing principle for ALP assay, the UV–vis spectra, fluorescent lifetime and zeta potential measurements were studied. In Fig. 4 A, the absorption peaks of DFQ and oxOPD display a large overlap with the excitation spectrum of Co-CDs near 365 nm. Compared to the superposition of Co-CDs and DFQ spectra (Fig. S9A), no peak disappears or appears with the addition of DFQ in Co-CDs system, and similar phenomena are also observed when oxOPD is introduced into Co-CDs system (Fig. S9B). The above results exclude static quenching effect in sensing system [42]. It indicates that the quenched fluorescent intensity of Co-CDs is caused possibly by IFE. The fluorescent lifetime of Co-CDs remains unchanged before and after the addition of DFQ and oxOPD (Fig. 4B), respectively, suggesting non-existence of the energy or electron transfer processes between Co-CDs and DFQ or oxOPD [43]. The zeta potentials of Co-CDs, DFQ and oxOPD are −22.71, −18.65, −24.4 mV (Fig. S10), respectively, and the electrostatic repulsion makes them apart from each other to block energy transfer [34]. Thus, it is feasible to apply Co-CDs nanozyme with fluorescent and oxidase-like property to sense ALP activity based on IFE to generate distinct fluorescent color variation from red and yellow to blue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of A-3D μPAD for ALP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To better detect ALP activity based on A-3D μPAD, the reaction parameters including reagent volume, concentration, incubation time and temperature were investigated. As shown in Fig. S11, the reagent volume is 18 μL to fill completely the sample zone of the bottom layer of A-3D μPAD. The hydrophobic barrier can avoid efficiently the fluid leakage and non-uniform distribution. With increasing of Co-CDs concentration, the oxidase-like activity rises resulting the elevation of R/B ratio (Fig. S12A). The R/B ratio also rises with OPD concentration ranging from 25 to 125 mM (Fig. S12B). Given the reagent consumption and sensitivity, the concentration of Co-CDs and OPD was set at 1.25 mg mL−1 and 100 mM, respectively. As exhibited the reaction conditions versus B/R ratio in Fig. S13, the B/R reaches maximum with AAP concentration at 75 m M (Fig. S13A), the B/R ratio responses highly at pH 8.5 (Fig. S13B), and the ALP/AAP/Co-CDs/OPD system achieves plateau at 25 min (Fig. S13C). Thus, the optimal conditions for ALP detection via A-3D μPAD were 75 mM AAP in Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 8.5) at 37 °C incubating for 25 min.

The performance of portable A-3D μPAD for ALP fluorometric determination was investigated under optimal conditions. As displayed in Fig. 5 , the fluorescent color of the detection zone changes from red and yellow to blue with increasing ALP activity (0–150 U L−1). By conversion of color signals into B/R ratios, a good linear relationship between ALP activity and B/R ratio is acquired in the range of 0.5–150 U L−1. The linear fitting equation is y = 0.0073x+0.9350 (R
2 0.9846) with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 U L−1 (S/N 3). The normal ALP level remains 40–130 U L−1 in health adults, and the clinical sample with high ALP activity should be diluted 3-fold to make the ALP activity fall into the linear range of this method. Thus, this method can meet sufficiently the demands for ALP monitoring in clinic. In Table S1, the total cost of A-3D μPAD for the test of serum ALP activity is about 0.395 (RMB), while the cost in the hospital is 17.1 (RMB). Compared with other reported strategies for ALP sensing, the established strategy exhibits higher sensitivity, wider linear range and lower LOD (Table S2). A-3D μPADs were stored for different days to evaluate the stability for ALP assay. As shown in Table S3, the linear ranges and LODs remain unchanged, and the detection results of A-3D μPADs are in agreement with the spiked ALP activity. The intra-assay and inter-assay coefficient of variation (CV) are less than 6.4 % and 9.2 %, respectively, indicating the high precision and stability of A-3D μPAD for ALP assay.

The common substances such as metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+), anions (Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−), small molecules (l-Glycine, L-Gly; Cholesterol, Chol; Glucose, Glu) and biomacromolecules (Glucose oxidase, GOX; Urate oxidase, UOX) were employed to evaluate the sensitivity and anti-interference ability of Co-CDs/OPD system for ALP detection. In Fig. 6 A, the B/R ratio is elevated obviously introducing ALP in Co-CDs/OPD system, while the B/R ratios remain unchanged with the addition of other substances. When the interfering substances were introduced into Co-CDs/OPD/ALP/AAP system, the B/R ratios remained constant (Fig. 6B). It implies these interfering substances have negligible influence on ALP detection. The excellent sensitivity and anti-interference ability are ascribed to the specificity of ALP and oxidase-mimicking property of Co-CDs. The stability of Co-CDs was investigated by long-time UV radiation (365 nm) and strong ionic strengths. As shown in Figs. S14A and B, the fluorescence intensity of Co-CDs displays no obvious change under UV radiation (120 min) and NaCl (1.2 M). These results indicate ALP/Co-CDs cascade system has great application potential for ALP detection in clinical disease diagnosis. Overall, the A-3D μPAD with merits of outstanding sensing performance, portable design, low cost and high-throughput analysis provides an alternative platform for ALP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP activity in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of A-3D μPAD for ALP assay, the whole human blood samples were directly detected without pretreatment. In Table S4, the detection results of above-mentioned method were kept consistent with biochemical detector in clinical laboratory and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 10 %. It indicates that A-3D μPAD-based method can be applied for accurate and reliable quantitative analysis of ALP level in real samples.

In conclusion, we presented an innovative fluorescent platform by Co-CDs/OPD system for facile, sensitive and accurate measurement of ALP activity in whole blood samples. The prepared Co-CDs nanozyme with high oxidase-mimicking activity could effectively oxidize OPD to oxOPD with the assistance of dissolved O2 in H2O, and combined with ALP/AAP system to fabricate cascade reaction. A-3D μPAD integrated with a portable detection device and a smartphone to enable on-site, economic and convenient ALP detection. The proposed sensor was feasible and reliable for ALP sensing in whole blood samples indicating that it was suitable for POCT implementation for timely diagnosis of related diseases. The μPAD-based POCT platforms will be indispensable diagnostic tools in health management in future. Moreover, this research will inspire the exploration of potential multifunctional nanozymes and nanozyme-mediated optical sensors in biochemical sensing fields.

Jing Guo: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jing Zhang: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xia Tong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202303021212159 and No. 202303021222319). This research was also supported by the Science Fundation of Shanxi Bethune Hospital (No. 2023GZRZ19 and No. 2023RC02).
